danger_signals.visual_form: Stoneheads and hostile creatures in tomb/ruin environments
danger_signals.meaning: Dangerous artifact-guarded locations; Lovecraftian threat presence

danger_signals.visual_form: Treacherous terrain in tombs, trains, forests, and Grand Canyon locations
danger_signals.meaning: World-state danger from both natural and supernatural sources

danger_signals.visual_form: Brady Tresidder encounters during idol search sequences
danger_signals.meaning: Competitive threat and potential world-destruction scenario

safety_signals.visual_form: Annet Myer sprite appearing alongside Earnest during progression
safety_signals.meaning: Reduced isolation; ally support in quest narrative

safety_signals.visual_form: Whip (primary) and situational weapons (exploding rocks, morning star, hammer)
safety_signals.meaning: Player capability increase; readiness against threats

safety_signals.visual_form: Advancement from Mexico to Peru to trains to forests to Grand Canyon
safety_signals.meaning: Forward momentum in world-saving quest; increasing player agency

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Artifact-seeking expedition presence across multiple continents (Mexico, Peru, North America)
Competitive presence in same locations; seeks same three idols for destructive purposes

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Hastur entity and cultist association (H.P. Lovecraft mythological reference)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Ancient ruins, tombs, artifact locations; mysterious figure Sigfried as intermediary

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: 1930s adventure aesthetic; historical setting with ancient artifact context
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Earnest Evans III inherits grandfather's unfinished quest; establishes narrative continuity across generations

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Multi-location environments (tombs in Mexico/Peru, moving trains, forest, Grand Canyon); artifact-guarded locations
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Racing against Brady Tresidder; time-pressure implicit in dual artifact-seeker narrative

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Lovecraftian horror elements; Hastur cult presence; mysterious Sigfried figure with unknown motives
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Urgency in idol collection; stakes escalate from treasure hunting to human survival

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Ragdoll-like fluid sprite animation of Earnest (multi-sprite construction); weapons appearing contextually in specific areas
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Character responsiveness to input; environmental weapon integration (exploding rocks in certain ruins, morning star in specific locations, hammer weapon for specialized puzzles)

environmental_storytelling.example: Journey through Mexico, Peru, moving trains, forest, and Grand Canyon communicates both geographical scope and escalating adventure intensity
Ancient tombs and ruins in Peru and Mexico establish that idols are protected by supernatural/environmental defenses
Meeting Annet Myer in Peruvian ruins shows that other survivors/allies exist within dangerous artifact zones
Brady Tresidder's pursuit across same locations signals competitive world-state and shared threat environment
Hastur cult references and mysterious Sigfried character visually embed cosmic horror into otherwise traditional treasure-hunting narrative

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Hostile engagement with whip-wielding protagonist; positioned as environmental hazards in tombs and ruins
Multi-sprite ragdoll-like movement suggesting acrobatic capability; weapon switching in context-specific locations indicates adaptability
Appears in progression sequence; accompanies Earnest suggesting collaborative narrative advancement
Competes for same artifacts; presence establishes time-pressure and dual-seeker world-state
Known connection to Hastur cult; presence signals supernatural plot layer beneath adventure narrative

notes: Earnest Evans is a 2D side-scrolling platform game that communicates world-state primarily through environmental location selection (tombs, trains, natural formations) rather than dynamic visual state changes. The game establishes danger through enemy design (stoneheads) and Lovecraftian narrative context (Hastur cult). Factions are communicated through character relationships and antagonist presence rather than visual faction markers. The game's Mega-CD version includes animated cutscenes by Madhouse that likely provide stronger narrative-to-visual communication than the Genesis version. The 1930s setting and artifact-recovery narrative structure establish a world-state of supernatural threat masked as adventure, with the Lovecraft literary connection providing horror-game semantics to platform gameplay. Multi-sprite ragdoll animation for the protagonist suggests emphasis on character physicality and environmental responsiveness as communication of player agency.
